preamble: The correspondence between the high front rounded vowel [y] (or the palatal glide [j]) and the voiceless velar fricative [x] is documented in historical sound shifts where glides correspond to back fricatives across morphological boundaries or in specific language families.

examples.note: Development of back fricatives in positions corresponding to historical palatal segments, often influenced by following vowels or syllable structure.
Proposed correspondences in historical reconstructions where front segments move backward toward the velum or uvula.

sources: Phonological research on Germanic and Austronesian fricative-glide correspondences.
